practice: Implement selection sort and trace it on a small array.
Implement insertion sort and explain why it is stable.
Compare when you would choose insertion sort vs a faster O(n log n) sort.
title: Algorithms Lesson 4: Sorting Basics
references: Introduction to Algorithms (Cormen et al.), Sorting Basics
summary: Understand how selection and insertion sort work and when they are useful.
content:
# Algorithms Lesson 4: Sorting Basics

## Overview
Sorting orders data so searching, grouping, and reporting become easier. We start with selection and insertion sort to build intuition.

## Selection sort
- Repeatedly selects the smallest remaining element
- Simple but always O(n^2) time
- Not stable in its typical swap-based form

## Insertion sort
- Builds a sorted prefix by inserting each next element
- O(n^2) worst case, but O(n) on nearly sorted data
- Stable when implemented by shifting elements

## Practical guidance
- Use insertion sort for tiny or nearly sorted arrays
- Prefer O(n log n) sorts for large inputs

## Key idea
Sorting tradeoffs often balance simplicity, stability, and performance.
examples: Sort a list of scores and keep duplicate order stable.
Explain why selection sort always does O(n^2) comparisons.
Identify which sort would be better for a nearly sorted list.
objectives: Describe how selection and insertion sort operate.
Compare their time complexity and stability.
Choose a simple sort for small input sizes.
Trace a sort step-by-step on sample data.
